perturbation.original_text: 11.4.1 In conducting the Clinical Trials and its other obligations under this Agreement and, if applicable, the Program Transfer Agreement, each Party will comply, and will use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to require each applicable Permitted Third Party of such Party to comply, with Applicable Laws relating to privacy or data protection applicable to such Party or the Clinical Trials being conducted by or on behalf of such Party, including ensuring that all necessary (a) consents from Clinical Investigators, Subjects and any others from whom Personally Identifiable Information will be received are obtained; (b) regulatory notifications are filed in all countries for which Sites have been selected; and (c) approvals are obtained in all countries for which Sites have been selected, prior to collection or transfer of such Personally Identifiable Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.4.1 In conducting the Clinical Trials and its other obligations under this Agreement and, if applicable, the Program Transfer Agreement, each Party will use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to comply with Applicable Laws relating to privacy or data protection. Each party will try to obtain consent from Clinical Investigators, Subjects and any others from whom Personally Identifiable Information will be received.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause ensures compliance with privacy laws, including obtaining consent for PII. The modified text weakens the consent requirement by changing 'will comply' to 'will use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to comply' and removing the mandatory aspect of obtaining consent. This weakens consumer rights regarding data privacy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: HIPAA mandates specific requirements for obtaining valid authorizations for the use and disclosure of protected health information (PHI). The modification weakens this requirement, potentially leading to non-compliance and violation of patient rights under HIPAA.

A limited data set is protected health information that excludes the following direct identifiers of the individual or of relatives, employers, or household members of the individual: (i) Names; (ii) Postal address information, other than town or city, State and zip code; (iii) Telephone numbers; (iv) Fax numbers; (v) Electronic mail addresses: (vi) Social security numbers; (vii) Medical record numbers; (viii) Health plan beneficiary numbers; (ix) Account numbers; (x) Certificate/license numbers; (xi) Vehicle identifiers and serial numbers, including license plate numbers; (xii) Device identifiers and serial numbers; (xiii) Web Universal Resource Locators (URLs); (xiv) Internet Protocol (IP) address numbers; (xv) Biometric identifiers, including finger and voice prints; (xvi) Full face photographic images and any comparable images. 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(e)(2). 44 45 C.F.R. § 164.508. 45 A covered entity may condition the provision of health care solely to generate protected health information for disclosure to a third party on the individual giving authorization to disclose the information to the third party. For example, a covered entity physician may condition the provision of a physical examination to be paid for by a life insurance iss
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with privacy laws, including obtaining consent for PII. The changed text weakens this requirement, making the consent aspect optional. The Perturbation Explanation states that this weakens consumer rights. The Law Explanation states that HIPAA mandates requirements for obtaining valid authorizations for use and disclosure of PHI. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 45 C.F.R. § 164.508 which outlines uses and disclosures for which authorization is required. By weakening the consent requirement, the modified text creates a contradiction of the law, as now a party can choose not to obtain consent where it is legally required.

perturbation.original_text: 12.3.1 Generally. Commencing as of the Effective Date and thereafter during the Development Term, and subject to Section 12.3.2 below, each Party will carry and maintain, at its own expense, insurance coverage of the kind and with liability limits that, at a minimum, satisfy the requirements of Section 12.3.2, to protect itself and the other Party against any claims or liabilities that may arise from the conduct of the Clinical Trials and all other rights and obligations hereunder with insurers with a minimum "A-" A.M. Best rating. Any deductibles for such insurance policies will be assumed by the insuring Party. Such insurance policies will be primary and non-contributing with respect to any other similar insurance policies available to other Party and their Affiliates. Prior to the Effective Date, and annually, at each anniversary of the Effective Date (unless, during such year, expiration of the applicable policy occurs first, in which case, on such expiration date), at a Party's written request the other Party will supply documentation of such insurance coverage via original certificates of insurance, if applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.3.1 Generally. Commencing as of the Effective Date and thereafter during the Development Term, and subject to Section 12.3.2 below, each Party will carry and maintain, at its own expense, insurance coverage of the kind and with liability limits to protect itself against any claims or liabilities that may arise from the conduct of the Clinical Trials and all other rights and obligations hereunder with insurers with a minimum "B-" A.M. Best rating. Any deductibles for such insurance policies will be assumed by the insuring Party. Prior to the Effective Date, at a Party's written request the other Party may supply documentation of such insurance coverage via certificates of insurance, if applicable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the minimum A.M. Best rating from 'A-' to 'B-' and replacing 'will' with 'may' weakens the clause, reducing the required level of insurance and making provision of documentation optional. Omitting that insurance should be primary and non-contributing further weakens liability and protection. This creates a legal contradiction with requirements of ensuring adequate financial responsibility for clinical trials.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 312.42 outlines the sponsor's responsibilities, implying the need to maintain adequate insurance or financial resources to cover potential liabilities arising from clinical trials. Weakening the insurance requirements may put patients at risk if the company does not have enough resources to cover unexpected events.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction by weakening insurance requirements and making documentation optional, potentially undermining the financial resources needed to cover liabilities from clinical trials. The original contract requires each party to 'carry and maintain' insurance and 'will supply documentation'. The modified contract uses 'may supply documentation', making it optional. The A.M. Best rating requirement is also lowered. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 21 CFR 312.42, supports the need for sponsors to have adequate resources for clinical trials. The change significantly weakens a legally mandated requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 15.16 Nonsolicitation. During the Term and for a period of [***] thereafter, neither Party shall solicit an employee of the other Party who is or has been involved in the performance or oversight of any of the development activities hereunder to terminate his or her employment and accept employment or work as a consultant with the soliciting Party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, nothing herein shall restrict or preclude the Parties' right to make generalized searches for employees by way of a general solicitation for employment placed in a trade journal, newspaper or website.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.16 Nonsolicitation. Neither Party shall solicit an employee of the other Party to terminate his or her employment and accept employment or work as a consultant with the soliciting Party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, nothing herein shall restrict or preclude the Parties' right to make generalized searches for employees.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause includes a time limitation (the '[***] thereafter' clause). Removing this limitation makes the nonsolicitation agreement indefinite, which may be deemed unenforceable due to restraint of trade principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While narrowly tailored nonsolicitation clauses can be enforceable, those that are overly broad or indefinite in duration are often deemed void. The removal of a time limitation makes the nonsolicitation clause arguably an unlawful restraint on trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which generally prohibits contracts restraining a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The original text included a time limitation on the nonsolicitation agreement, while the modified text removes this limitation, making the nonsolicitation agreement indefinite. According to the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, indefinite nonsolicitation clauses are often deemed void. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By removing the time limit, the modified contract arguably creates an unlawful restraint on trade, contradicting the law.
